Green skills refer to the knowledge, abilities, values and attitudes needed to live in, develop and support a sustainable and resource-efficient society.
The mission is implemented by the Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship (MSDE).
PMKVY is the flagship scheme under the Skill India Mission.
mainsPoints: Discuss the necessity and potential impact of 'Skill India Mission 2.0' in addressing India's unemployment challenge and leveraging its demographic dividend.
Analyze the importance of integrating green skills and digital literacy into national skill development programs for a sustainable and future-ready workforce.
Evaluate the role of public-private partnerships in achieving the objectives of large-scale skill development initiatives.
Examine the challenges in implementing skill development programs effectively across diverse geographical and socio-economic contexts in India.
GS2.imageDescription: Young Indian individuals engaged in hands-on training for green technology and digital skills.
GS2.trivia: India has one of the youngest populations in the world, with over 65% of its population under 35 years of age.

GS2.headline: Quad Nations Conclude Joint Naval Drills, Emphasizing Maritime Security in Indo-Pacific
GS2.explainer:
• The Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (Quad) nations — India, the United States, Japan, and Australia — have successfully concluded a series of joint naval exercises.
• The drills focused on enhancing interoperability, information sharing, and coordinated responses to regional security challenges in the Indo-Pacific.
• The exercises covered anti-submarine warfare, air defence, and maritime interdiction operations, reinforcing a free and open Indo-Pacific vision.
• This collaboration underscores the Quad's commitment to upholding international law and promoting stability in a strategically vital region.
• The participants reiterated their resolve to counter illicit activities and ensure freedom of navigation in international waters.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
Security challenges and their management in border areas
prelimsPoints: The Quad is an informal strategic forum comprising India, Japan, Australia, and the United States.
Its primary objective is to work for a free, open, prosperous, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region.
The Malabar naval exercise, originally between India and the US, expanded to include Japan (2015) and Australia (2020), becoming a key Quad military activity.
The Indo-Pacific is a biogeographic region encompassing the Indian Ocean and the western and central Pacific Ocean.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of the Quad in shaping the geopolitical landscape of the Indo-Pacific region.
Discuss how joint naval drills contribute to maritime security and stability, particularly in addressing challenges like piracy, terrorism, and territorial disputes.
Evaluate India's role and interests within the Quad framework and its implications for India's foreign policy and security posture.
Examine the criticisms and concerns surrounding the Quad's objectives and its potential impact on regional power dynamics.
GS2.imageDescription: Four warships from different navies sailing in formation during a joint exercise in open waters.
GS2.trivia: The idea of the Quad was first mooted by Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe in 2007.

GS2.headline: E-Governance Initiatives Expand to Remote Rural Areas, Boosting Digital Inclusion and Service Delivery
GS2.explainer:
• The government has announced the successful expansion of various e-governance platforms and digital services to previously underserved remote rural areas.
• This push aims to enhance digital inclusion, bridge the rural-urban digital divide, and improve the efficiency and transparency of public service delivery.
• Services like online land records, digital health consultations, e-education platforms, and direct benefit transfers are now more accessible to rural citizens.
• The initiative leverages common service centers (CSCs) and BharatNet infrastructure to ensure last-mile connectivity and digital literacy.
• This expansion is seen as a critical step towards realizing the vision of 'Digital India' and empowering rural communities.
gsSubTopics: E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential
Citizen Charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: Digital India is a flagship program of the Government of India with a vision to transform India into a digitally empowered society and knowledge economy.
BharatNet project aims to provide broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats.

Common Service Centers (CSCs) are access points for delivery of essential public utility services, social welfare schemes, healthcare, financial, education and agriculture services to citizens in rural and remote areas of the country.
E-governance aims to make government services accessible to citizens in a convenient, efficient, and transparent manner.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of e-governance initiatives in bridging the rural-urban divide and empowering rural communities in India.
Analyze the challenges in ensuring universal digital inclusion and effective service delivery in remote and diverse geographical regions.
Evaluate the role of infrastructure projects like BharatNet and community-level initiatives like CSCs in the success of e-governance.
Suggest measures to further enhance the adoption and impact of digital services, addressing issues of digital literacy and cybersecurity in rural areas.
GS2.imageDescription: A rural Indian family accessing government services on a tablet device, connected via a digital network.
GS2.trivia: The 'Digital India' program was launched on July 1, 2015.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Committee Recommends Sweeping Reforms in Electoral Funding and Campaign Finance
GS2.explainer:
• A bipartisan Parliamentary Standing Committee has submitted a report recommending significant reforms to electoral funding and campaign finance laws.
• Key recommendations include increasing transparency in political donations, reviewing the electoral bond scheme, and capping election expenditure by political parties.
• The committee highlighted concerns over the role of 'dark money' in elections and its potential impact on democratic processes.
• The report calls for a stronger regulatory framework to ensure a level playing field and reduce corporate influence in politics.
• These proposals aim to enhance public trust in the electoral system and strengthen democratic accountability.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these
Pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity
prelimsPoints: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is an autonomous constitutional authority responsible for administering election processes in India.
Electoral bonds are instruments through which individuals and corporate entities can donate money to political parties.
The Representation of People's Act, 1951, governs the conduct of elections in India.
Various committees like the Dinesh Goswami Committee and the Indrajit Gupta Committee have recommended electoral reforms.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the existing framework of electoral funding in India, specifically addressing the concerns raised about transparency and accountability.
Discuss the potential impact of the recommended reforms on the democratic process, political party functioning, and the role of money in elections.
Analyze the arguments for and against the electoral bond scheme and propose alternative mechanisms for transparent political funding.
Examine the role of the Election Commission of India and parliamentary committees in driving electoral reforms and strengthening democratic institutions.
GS2.imageDescription: A ballot box with Indian currency notes, symbolizing the issue of money in elections.
GS2.trivia: The first general elections in independent India were held from 1951-52.

GS2.headline: India Participates in G20 Sherpa Meeting, Shaping Agenda for Upcoming Leaders' Summit
GS2.explainer:
• India's Sherpa is actively participating in the ongoing G20 Sherpa meeting, contributing significantly to the agenda-setting for the upcoming Leaders' Summit.
• Discussions are centered on critical global issues including climate finance, sustainable development goals, digital public infrastructure, and multilateral reforms.
• India is advocating for a 'human-centric globalization' approach, emphasizing equitable access to resources and technology for developing nations.
• The meeting aims to finalize the draft declaration and key deliverables that will be presented to the heads of state and government.
• This engagement underscores India's growing influence in global governance and its commitment to addressing shared challenges.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The G20 is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union (EU).
It works to address major issues related to the global economy, such as international financial stability, climate change mitigation, and sustainable development.
A Sherpa is a personal representative of the head of state or government of a G20 member country at international summits.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023, focusing on 'Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam' or 'One Earth, One Family, One Future'.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's active participation in the G20 forum in shaping global economic and developmental agendas.
Analyze India's key priorities and contributions to discussions on climate finance, sustainable development, and digital public infrastructure within the G20 framework.
Evaluate the role of multilateral institutions like the G20 in addressing complex global challenges and fostering international cooperation.
Examine the concept of 'human-centric globalization' and its relevance in the current geopolitical and economic context.
GS2.imageDescription: G20 logo displayed prominently during a high-level international meeting.
GS2.trivia: The G20 was formed in 1999 in response to the Asian financial crisis.

GS2.headline: National Education Policy Implementation Review Highlights Digital Divide Challenges
GS2.explainer:
• An interim review of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 implementation has pointed out significant challenges, particularly concerning the digital divide.
• While the policy aims for universal access to quality education, disparities in access to digital infrastructure and devices continue to hinder its effective reach in remote and economically disadvantaged regions.
• The review committee emphasized the need for targeted interventions to ensure equitable access to online learning resources and digital literacy for all students and educators.
• Recommendations include increased investment in public digital infrastructure, provision of affordable devices, and comprehensive teacher training in digital pedagogies.
• The NEP's ambitious goals require addressing these foundational inequalities to ensure inclusive educational outcomes.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 aims to transform India's education system to meet the needs of the 21st century.
It replaces the National Policy on Education of 1986.
Key features include a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure, multidisciplinary education, vocational education integration, and emphasis on digital education.
The digital divide refers to the gap between demographics and regions that have access to modern information and communications technology, and those that don't.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and key features of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 and its vision for transforming Indian education.
Analyze the challenges posed by the digital divide in the effective implementation of NEP 2020, particularly in achieving equitable access to quality education.
Evaluate the government's strategies and initiatives to bridge the digital divide in education and promote digital literacy across all sections of society.
Suggest comprehensive measures to address the infrastructural, financial, and pedagogical barriers to inclusive digital education in India.
GS2.imageDescription: A diverse group of students in a classroom setting, some using digital tablets, others with traditional books, highlighting the digital divide.
GS2.trivia: The first National Policy on Education was promulgated in 1968.

GS2.headline: Annual Talks on Indus Waters Treaty Conclude Amidst Regional Water Scarcity Concerns
GS2.explainer:
• The annual meeting of the Permanent Indus Commission, comprising officials from India and Pakistan, concluded with discussions on various aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT).
• The talks focused on sharing of hydrological data, future projects on the Western rivers, and resolving minor technical disputes, against a backdrop of increasing regional water scarcity.
• Both sides reiterated their commitment to the treaty, which has survived multiple conflicts and remains a cornerstone of water sharing between the two nations.
• The ongoing climate change impacts, including glacier melt and erratic rainfall patterns, underscore the urgency of cooperation on water management.
• While no major breakthroughs were announced, the continuation of dialogue is seen as positive for regional stability.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) was signed in 1960 between India and Pakistan, brokered by the World Bank.
It allocates the waters of the Indus river system, giving India control over the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) and Pakistan control over the Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab).
The Permanent Indus Commission is a bilateral commission of experts from India and Pakistan, established under the IWT.
The treaty provides for a mechanism for cooperation and information exchange regarding the use of rivers, and for the settlement of disputes.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical significance and enduring relevance of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) in India-Pakistan relations, despite geopolitical tensions.
Analyze the challenges posed by climate change, increasing water demand, and upstream-downstream riparian issues to the future of transboundary water sharing in South Asia.
Evaluate the mechanisms within the IWT for dispute resolution and information exchange, and suggest ways to enhance cooperation on water management.
Examine the broader implications of water scarcity and water diplomacy for regional stability and security in the Indian subcontinent.
GS2.imageDescription: A map showing the Indus River system flowing through India and Pakistan.
GS2.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty is considered one of the most successful water-sharing treaties in the world.

GS2.headline: Microfinance Sector Plays Crucial Role in Women's Economic Empowerment, Says NABARD Report
GS2.explainer:
• A new report by the National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD) highlights the significant role of the microfinance sector in fostering women's economic empowerment in rural India.
• The report showcases how access to small loans and financial literacy through Self-Help Groups (SHGs) has improved livelihoods, increased income generation, and enhanced decision-making power for women.
• It emphasizes the need for continued policy support and innovative financial products to further strengthen the microfinance ecosystem.
• Despite challenges like over-indebtedness, the sector has demonstrated resilience and played a vital role in poverty alleviation and financial inclusion.
• The findings underscore microfinance as a key instrument in achieving gender equality and inclusive growth objectives.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
prelimsPoints: Microfinance refers to a range of financial services, such as small loans, savings, and insurance, provided to low-income individuals or groups.
Self-Help Groups (SHGs) are informal associations of people who come together to find ways to improve their living conditions.
NABARD (National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development) is an apex development financial institution in India, primarily for rural development.
The SHG-Bank Linkage Programme is a key model for microfinance in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative impact of microfinance and Self-Help Groups (SHGs) on women's economic empowerment and social development in rural India.
Analyze the challenges faced by the microfinance sector, such as regulatory issues, over-indebtedness, and sustainability, and suggest policy measures to address them.
Evaluate the role of NABARD and other financial institutions in promoting financial inclusion and supporting the microfinance ecosystem.
Examine how microfinance can be further leveraged to achieve broader goals of poverty alleviation, gender equality, and sustainable rural livelihoods.
GS2.imageDescription: A group of rural women engaging in a microfinance meeting, discussing their small business ventures.
GS2.trivia: Nobel Peace Prize laureate Muhammad Yunus is known as the father of microfinance.

GS2.headline: India Continues to Provide Humanitarian Aid to Afghanistan, Reiterates Call for Inclusive Government
GS2.explainer:
• India has dispatched another consignment of humanitarian assistance, including food grains and medical supplies, to the people of Afghanistan.
• This aid delivery underscores India's commitment to supporting the Afghan population amidst ongoing economic and humanitarian crises.
• Simultaneously, India reiterated its stance on the need for an inclusive government in Afghanistan that respects the rights of all citizens, particularly women and minorities.
• The aid is being channeled through UN agencies and other international partners to ensure equitable distribution.
• India maintains that long-term stability in Afghanistan is crucial for regional peace and security.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India has historically been a significant development partner for Afghanistan.
After the Taliban takeover in August 2021, India has primarily focused on humanitarian assistance.
India's policy towards Afghanistan is guided by UN Security Council Resolution 2593 (2021), which calls for an inclusive political settlement.
The Chabahar Port in Iran is crucial for India to send aid to Afghanistan, bypassing Pakistan.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's evolving foreign policy approach towards Afghanistan since the Taliban takeover and the rationale behind its continued humanitarian assistance.
Analyze the challenges faced by India in balancing humanitarian concerns with its strategic interests in a politically unstable Afghanistan.
Evaluate the importance of an inclusive government in Afghanistan for regional peace, human rights, and the prevention of terrorism.
Examine the role of international cooperation and multilateral platforms in addressing the humanitarian crisis and fostering long-term stability in Afghanistan.
GS2.imageDescription: Bags of food grains with Indian and Afghan flags, symbolizing humanitarian aid.
GS2.trivia: India has built the Afghan Parliament building and the Salma Dam (Friendship Dam) in Afghanistan.

GS3.headline: ISRO Prepares for Launch of Next-Generation NavIC Satellite for Enhanced Positioning Services
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) is in advanced stages of preparation for the launch of a next-generation satellite in its NavIC constellation.
• This new satellite aims to further enhance the accuracy, availability, and robustness of India's indigenous navigation system, NavIC (Navigation with Indian Constellation).
• The improved capabilities will benefit critical sectors such as disaster management, public vehicle tracking, precise timing, and military applications.
• The launch underscores India's commitment to self-reliance in space technology and reducing dependence on foreign GPS systems.
• This mission is part of ISRO's ongoing efforts to modernize its satellite infrastructure and expand the reach of its services.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Space technology
Applications of space technology
prelimsPoints: NavIC is India's own regional satellite navigation system, officially known as IRNSS.
It consists of 7 satellites (3 in GEO and 4 in GSO), covering India and a region extending up to 1500 km around it.
NavIC provides two types of services: Standard Position Service (SPS) for civilian users and Restricted Service (RS) for authorized users (military).
The system operates on L5 and S-band frequencies.
India is one of only a few countries with its own satellite navigation system.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of an indigenous satellite navigation system like NavIC for India's national security and economic development.
Analyze the potential applications of enhanced NavIC services in various sectors, including disaster management, transportation, and agriculture.
Evaluate India's progress in achieving self-reliance in space technology and its implications for global space leadership.
Examine the challenges in maintaining and upgrading satellite navigation systems and how ISRO is addressing them.
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of an ISRO satellite orbiting Earth, with signals emanating towards the Indian subcontinent.
GS3.trivia: NavIC was declared operational in 2018 and is designed to provide accurate position information service to users in India and the region.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Repo Rate, Signals Cautious Stance Amidst Evolving Economic Outlook
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) has decided to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged in its latest review.
• This decision reflects a cautious approach, balancing persistent inflationary pressures with the need to support economic growth.
• The RBI highlighted global uncertainties, crude oil price volatility, and domestic food inflation as key factors influencing its policy stance.
• Analysts suggest the central bank is adopting a 'wait and watch' approach, prioritizing price stability while monitoring growth impulses.
• Forward guidance indicates a data-dependent approach, with potential for future adjustments based on inflation trajectory and economic recovery.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary policy
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate (repo rate) in India.
The repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
The primary objective of the MPC is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Inflation targeting framework in India aims to keep CPI inflation at 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate amidst current economic conditions, considering both inflationary concerns and growth imperatives.
Discuss the tools and objectives of monetary policy in India and their effectiveness in achieving macroeconomic stability.
Evaluate the impact of global economic uncertainties and geopolitical events on India's monetary policy decisions.
Examine the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing price stability and supporting economic recovery, particularly in the context of supply-side shocks.
GS3.imageDescription: A close-up shot of the Reserve Bank of India logo on a building, symbolizing economic policy.
GS3.trivia: The RBI was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

GS3.headline: New Report Highlights Intensified Threats to Western Ghats Biodiversity from Infrastructure Projects and Climate Change
GS3.explainer:
• A comprehensive report by a consortium of environmental organizations has raised alarm over the escalating threats to the biodiversity of the Western Ghats.
• The report identifies unchecked infrastructure development, unregulated tourism, mining, and the impacts of climate change as primary drivers of habitat loss and species decline.
• It calls for stricter enforcement of environmental regulations and a review of ongoing projects within the ecologically sensitive zones (ESZs) of the region.
• The Western Ghats, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is one of the world's eight 'hottest hotspots' of biological diversity.
• Recommendations include sustainable land-use planning, community-based conservation, and enhanced scientific monitoring to protect this unique ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Biodiversity
Climate change
prelimsPoints: The Western Ghats is a mountain range that runs parallel to the western coast of the Indian peninsula.
It is recognized as one of the world's eight 'hottest hotspots' of biological diversity.
It is home to a large proportion of India's flora and fauna, many of which are endemic.
The Madhav Gadgil Committee (Western Ghats Ecology Expert Panel) and the K. Kasturirangan Committee reports have made recommendations for its conservation.
Four states primarily covered are Gujarat, Maharashtra, Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological significance of the Western Ghats and the various threats it faces from human activities and climate change.
Analyze the effectiveness of current conservation policies and regulatory frameworks in protecting biodiversity hotspots in India.
Propose sustainable development models that can reconcile economic growth with environmental conservation in ecologically fragile regions like the Western Ghats.
Examine the challenges in implementing recommendations from expert committees (like Gadgil and Kasturirangan) for the Western Ghats.
GS3.imageDescription: Lush green landscape of the Western Ghats with a diverse array of flora and fauna.
GS3.trivia: The Western Ghats were declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2012.

GS3.headline: Government Announces New Incentive Package to Boost Growth in MSME Sector
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Micro, Small & Medium Enterprises (MSME) has rolled out a new comprehensive incentive package aimed at bolstering the growth and resilience of the sector.
• The package includes enhanced credit facilities, easier access to technology upgrades, and simplified regulatory compliance measures.
• The objective is to stimulate employment generation, foster innovation, and increase the contribution of MSMEs to India's GDP.
• Special provisions have been made to support women entrepreneurs and MSMEs in backward regions, promoting inclusive growth.
• This move is critical as MSMEs are considered the backbone of the Indian economy, accounting for a significant share of employment and exports.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
Industrial policy
prelimsPoints: MSMEs are defined based on investment in plant & machinery/equipment and turnover.
The MSME sector contributes significantly to India's GDP, employment, and exports.
Major government initiatives for MSMEs include Mudra Yojana, Emergency Credit Line Guarantee Scheme (ECLGS), and Champions Portal.
UDYAM Registration is the new process for registering MSMEs.
mainsPoints: Discuss the critical role of the MSME sector in India's economic development and its potential for inclusive growth and employment generation.
Analyze the key challenges faced by MSMEs in India, such as access to finance, technology, and market, and how the new incentive package addresses them.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government policies and schemes in promoting the growth and competitiveness of the MSME sector.
Suggest further measures to strengthen MSMEs, particularly focusing on their integration into global value chains and resilience against economic shocks.
GS3.imageDescription: A diverse group of entrepreneurs in a workshop setting, representing the MSME sector.
GS3.trivia: The MSME sector accounts for about 30% of India's GDP and employs over 11 crore people.

GS3.headline: Indian Researchers Develop AI-Powered Diagnostics for Early Detection of Non-Communicable Diseases
GS3.explainer:
• A team of Indian scientists and AI experts has developed a groundbreaking AI-powered diagnostic tool for the early and accurate detection of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs).
• The technology utilizes machine learning algorithms to analyze vast amounts of patient data, including medical images, genetic information, and lifestyle factors.
• This innovation promises to revolutionize healthcare by enabling proactive disease management and improving patient outcomes, especially in remote areas.
• The tool is currently undergoing clinical trials and has shown high accuracy in identifying early markers for cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, and certain cancers.
• This development aligns with India's national health mission to combat the rising burden of NCDs.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
Health
prelimsPoints: NCDs (e.g., heart disease, stroke, cancer, diabetes, chronic respiratory diseases) are the leading causes of death globally.
AI and ML are subsets of computer science that enable systems to learn from data, identify patterns, and make decisions.
India faces a significant burden of NCDs, accounting for a large proportion of deaths and disabilities.
The National Health Mission (NHM) aims to achieve universal access to equitable, affordable and quality healthcare services.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and machine learning in transforming healthcare delivery and disease management in India.
Analyze how early detection of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) can alleviate the public health burden and improve the quality of life.
Evaluate the ethical considerations and challenges associated with the widespread adoption of AI in medical diagnostics, including data privacy and algorithmic bias.
Examine the steps India needs to take to integrate advanced technologies like AI into its public healthcare system effectively.
GS3.imageDescription: A doctor reviewing medical scans on a screen, with AI interface elements overlayed.
GS3.trivia: Globally, NCDs kill 41 million people each year, equivalent to 74% of all deaths.

GS3.headline: India Bolsters Cybersecurity Framework Following Recent High-Profile Data Breaches
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian government has announced a series of measures to strengthen its national cybersecurity framework in response to a surge in sophisticated cyberattacks and data breaches.
• The new directives include mandatory reporting of cyber incidents, enhanced data protection protocols for critical infrastructure, and increased investment in cybersecurity research and development.
• CERT-In has been tasked with greater powers for incident response and coordination with public and private entities.
• The move aims to protect sensitive citizen data, secure government digital assets, and safeguard critical national infrastructure from evolving cyber threats.
• Public awareness campaigns on cybersecurity best practices are also being rolled out nationwide.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
prelimsPoints: CERT-In is the national agency for responding to computer security incidents as and when they occur.

Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) refers to information infrastructure, the incapacitation or destruction of which would have a debilitating impact on national security, economy, public health or safety.
India's IT Act, 2000, and the new Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act) provide legal frameworks for cybersecurity and data protection.
The National Cyber Security Policy 2013 aims to protect information infrastructure and enhance national cyber capabilities.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving landscape of cyber threats in India and the challenges in safeguarding critical infrastructure and citizen data.
Analyze the key provisions and impact of the strengthened cybersecurity framework, including mandatory reporting and data protection protocols.
Evaluate the role of various stakeholders, including government agencies, private sector, and individuals, in building a resilient national cybersecurity posture.
Suggest measures to enhance international cooperation in combating cybercrime and improving India's offensive and defensive cyber capabilities.
GS3.imageDescription: A digital padlock icon overlaid on a map of India, representing national cybersecurity.
GS3.trivia: The first computer virus was created in the early 1970s.

GS3.headline: Government Unveils New Policy Framework for Promoting Circular Economy in India
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has introduced a comprehensive policy framework aimed at accelerating India's transition to a circular economy.
• The policy focuses on reducing waste, promoting reuse and recycling, and extending the lifecycle of products across various sectors, including electronics, plastics, and construction.
• It outlines incentives for businesses adopting circular practices, research and development in sustainable materials, and public awareness campaigns.
• The initiative is crucial for achieving India's climate targets, conserving natural resources, and fostering green growth.
• This framework seeks to integrate circularity into national production and consumption patterns, moving away from a linear 'take-make-dispose' model.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Waste management
Sustainable development
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: A circular economy is an economic system aimed at eliminating waste and the continual use of resources.
It contrasts with a linear economy (take, make, dispose) model.
Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) is a policy approach under which producers are given a significant responsibility for the treatment or disposal of post-consumer products.
India's waste management rules (e.g., Plastic Waste Management Rules, E-Waste Management Rules) are steps towards circularity.
mainsPoints: Discuss the concept of a circular economy and its significance in addressing environmental degradation, resource depletion, and climate change challenges in India.
Analyze the key features of India's new policy framework for promoting a circular economy and its potential impact on various industrial sectors.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for businesses and consumers in transitioning from a linear to a circular economic model.
Suggest policy interventions and technological advancements required to effectively implement circular economy principles at scale in India.
GS3.imageDescription: An infographic illustrating the circular economy model with arrows showing reuse, recycle, and repair.
GS3.trivia: The term 'circular economy' gained prominence with the Ellen MacArthur Foundation's work.

GS3.headline: India Achieves Milestone in Indigenous Vaccine Development for Emerging Tropical Diseases
GS3.explainer:
• Indian pharmaceutical companies and research institutions have announced a significant breakthrough in the indigenous development of a vaccine for a prevalent tropical disease.
• This achievement highlights India's growing capabilities in biotechnology and its commitment to addressing public health challenges specific to tropical regions.
• The vaccine, developed through collaborative research, has shown promising results in initial clinical trials, demonstrating high efficacy and safety profiles.
• This development is crucial for enhancing India's vaccine self-reliance and contributing to global health security, especially for diseases neglected by larger markets.
• Experts project that the vaccine could be rolled out for widespread use within the next two years, pending final regulatory approvals.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Health
prelimsPoints: India is known as the 'pharmacy of the world' due to its large-scale vaccine and pharmaceutical production.
Tropical diseases, also known as Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs), are prevalent in tropical and subtropical regions.
The Department of Biotechnology (DBT) and Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) play key roles in vaccine R&D.
Clinical trials are research studies performed on human volunteers to assess the safety and efficacy of new drugs or vaccines.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's role as a global leader in vaccine manufacturing and its increasing capabilities in indigenous vaccine development for emerging diseases.
Analyze the importance of developing vaccines for tropical diseases, often neglected by global pharmaceutical giants, in addressing public health disparities.
Evaluate the collaborative ecosystem of government, research institutions, and private industry that drives India's advancements in biotechnology and healthcare innovations.
Examine the challenges in accelerating vaccine development, ensuring equitable access, and strengthening regulatory frameworks for public health interventions in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A scientist in a lab coat holding a vial, with advanced laboratory equipment in the background.
GS3.trivia: India produces 60% of the world's vaccines and 20% of generic drugs.

GS3.headline: Government Boosts Food Grain Storage Capacity to Enhance National Food Security
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Government has announced significant investments to expand and modernize India's food grain storage capacity across the country.
• This initiative aims to minimize post-harvest losses, ensure efficient distribution, and strengthen national food security, particularly for vulnerable populations.
• The expansion includes the construction of new warehouses, upgrading existing facilities with advanced technology, and promoting decentralized storage solutions.
• This move is critical in the context of climate change impacts on agriculture and ensuring buffer stocks for public distribution systems.
• The enhanced storage infrastructure will support farmers by providing better post-harvest management and reducing distress sales.
gsSubTopics: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers
Food processing and related industries in India- scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management
Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security
GS3.source: Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food & Public Distribution
prelimsPoints: The Food Corporation of India (FCI) is the primary agency responsible for procuring, storing, and distributing food grains for the PDS.
India maintains buffer stocks of food grains (wheat and rice) to ensure food security and price stability.
Post-harvest losses are a significant challenge in Indian agriculture, leading to wastage of food grains.
The National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013, aims to provide subsidized food grains to approximately two-thirds of India's population.
mainsPoints: Discuss the critical role of efficient food grain storage and supply chain management in ensuring national food security and minimizing post-harvest losses in India.
Analyze the challenges faced by India's public distribution system (PDS) and the potential impact of enhanced storage capacity on its effectiveness.
Evaluate the government's strategies to modernize agricultural infrastructure and support farmers through better post-harvest management practices.
Examine the linkages between climate change, agricultural productivity, and the need for robust food grain storage systems to build resilience in the food supply chain.
